title: Karpov's Caro-Kann
note: Karpov, the great Caro-Kann specialist, outplays Spassky from the black side.

title: Smyslov vs Botvinnik, WC
note: A World Championship Caro-Kann — classical maneuvering at the highest level.

title: The Evergreen Game
note: Anderssen sacrifices for a storming kingside attack culminating in a famous queen sacrifice.

title: Botvinnik's French
note: Botvinnik defends the French with characteristic strategic depth and counterattacks.

title: Tal vs Botvinnik, WC 1960
note: From their World Championship match — Tal's dynamic handling of the French Winawer.

title: The Game of the Century
note: 13-year-old Fischer's immortal — the windmill and queen sacrifice vs Donald Byrne.

title: The Immortal Game
note: Anderssen gives up both rooks and the queen to deliver mate with his minor pieces.

title: The Opera Game
note: Morphy's model attack — rapid development, open lines, and a clean mating finish at the opera house.

4.explanation: Morphy opens the center immediately. With …d6 weakening Black's pawn chain, d4 strikes at the heart of the position and prepares to exploit the pin on g4.

alternatives.note: Bc4 develops toward f7, but Morphy plays d4 first — opening the center is the priority when Black's structure is already loose.
Nc3 is natural development, but d4 challenges the center right away and sets up the dxe5 tactic on the next move.
Be2 is too passive. Morphy's whole plan is rapid, open play — strike in the center with d4.

6.explanation: dxe5! Morphy wins a pawn and opens the position. The pawn grab also threatens to trap the bishop on g4 if Black tries to hold the pawn.

alternatives.note: Bc4 is a good developing move, but there is something more forcing — capture on e5 first to win material and open lines.
Nc3 develops, but dxe5 is more urgent. You win a pawn and open the position while the bishop on g4 is still pinned to the queen.
h3 challenges the bishop, but after …Bxf3 your pawn structure is damaged. Take on e5 first — it's more forcing and wins a pawn.
Be2 is passive. Morphy strikes in the center with dxe5, grabbing material and opening the game.

8.explanation: Qxf3 recaptures with the queen, keeping a powerful attacking piece aimed at f7. Morphy avoids the doubled f-pawns that gxf3 would create.

alternatives.note: gxf3 recaptures but leaves doubled, isolated f-pawns. The queen recapture keeps your structure intact and eyes the weak f7 square.
Bc4 develops, but you must recapture on f3 first. The queen on f3 is a powerful attacking piece — don't leave it behind.

10.explanation: Bc4 develops with a threat — the bishop and queen both target f7. Morphy develops every move with purpose, always creating threats.

alternatives.note: Be2 is safe but slow. Bc4 adds immediate pressure on f7 and prepares the queen to jump to b3.
Nc3 develops, but Bc4 first creates an immediate threat on f7 — development with a purpose beats plain development.

12.explanation: Qb3! A classic Morphy move — the queen attacks b7 and eyes f7 at the same time. Black's undeveloped pieces can't keep up.

alternatives.note: Qd3 is less active. Qb3 hits b7 and f7 simultaneously — Morphy always chooses the move that creates the most threats.
Castling is normally sound, but Qb3 creates immediate threats before Black can finish developing.

18.explanation: Nxb5! Morphy sacrifices the knight to rip open Black's position. The pawn on b5 was the only thing holding Black's center together.

alternatives.note: Bxf6 damages Black's pawn structure but misses the point. The knight sacrifice on b5 opens lines and wins back material with interest.
Qxb5 takes the pawn, but after …Qxb5 you've traded queens and lost the attack. The knight sacrifice keeps the initiative.
Bxb5 wins a pawn safely, but Morphy's knight sacrifice is more forcing — it opens the position and keeps every piece in the attack.

24.explanation: Rxd7! Morphy sacrifices the exchange to remove Black's last defender. The rook on d7 was the only piece holding the position together.

alternatives.label: Also winning
alternatives.note: Bxd7+ wins the exchange, but Morphy's rook sacrifice is cleaner — it removes the defender and keeps the attack rolling toward mate.

title: Kasparov's Immortal
note: Kasparov vs Topalov, Wijk aan Zee — a breathtaking king hunt after a rook sacrifice.

0.explanation: Kasparov has won many brilliant attacking games with both 1.e4 and 1.d4. Here he pursues an open game.

1.explanation: Kasparov is probably THE greatest opening theoretician of all time so it makes sense to avoid main lines against him. Topalov selects the less common Pirc Defense, but now he runs the risk of getting in trouble not because he is outprepared, but because the opening may be slightly dubious at the highest level.

2.explanation: It is of course correct to establish the "perfect pawn center" against almost any hypermodern opening.

5.explanation: Topalov commits to the true "Pirc" defense with a kingside fianchetto.

6.explanation: As is his way, Kasparov selects an aggressive setup, developing the bishop to pursue a quick queen and bishop battery and castle queenside.

8.explanation: This move already prepares queenside castling and Bh6.

9.explanation: An intriguing and flexible move! Rather than castling, Topalov adopts a very flexible expansion on the queenside.

alternatives.label: Dubious
alternatives.note: Exchanging the dark-square bishop would be a huge strategic achievement, but Topalov would only overextend if he tried this move. The dark-square bishop survives, and Black's advanced kingside pawns may easily become targets.
Naturally Topalov avoids castling into the attack.

10.explanation: This is a very useful (often essential) move in such positions. Kasparov secures e4 and keeps a knight out of g4.

12.explanation: This move is slightly odd since the knight has nowhere good to go right away and blocks the Bf1.

alternatives.note: Stockfish prefers developing the bishop.

13.explanation: The knight develops to its most natural square, controlling e5 and c5.

14.explanation: A very committal decision from Kasparov. He elects to exchange the "dragon bishop," but he must sideline his queen.

15.explanation: Topalov pulls Kasparov's queen to h6, where it is misplaced.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: This would be very risky as Kasparov can immediately launch a kingside attack.

17.explanation: The bishop develops and adds important control to the d5-square.

18.explanation: Kasparov takes a moment to stop ...b4.

alternatives.note: Many beginners might play this move. White can only retreat and nothing was accomplished.

19.explanation: Topalov finally makes a control commitment. He also shows his plan, ...Qe7 and 0-0-0.

alternatives.label: Interesting
alternatives.note: The engine preferred more queenside expansion.

20.explanation: Now that Topalov has committed, Kasparov does too.

21.explanation: Topalov places the queen in a perfect spot.

22.explanation: A grandmaster move! Kasparov is patient and places his king on the safer b1-square.

24.explanation: The knight repositions and frees the f1-bishop.

26.explanation: The knight finishes its journey and has a possible new target, a5.

27.explanation: Topalov decides it's time to release the central tensions.

28.explanation: Recapturing with the knight was of roughly equal value.

29.explanation: The b7-bishop is released with a gain of tempo on the rook.

30.explanation: The rook retreats to the first rank.

31.explanation: This one move activates both the knight and the Rd8 in the fight for d5.

32.explanation: Kasparov needs to activate his f1-bishop and h1-rook so he prepares Bh3.

34.explanation: Kasparov plays to trade off the b7-bishop, but Topalov can let him have it and use the time gained to break in the center.

alternatives.note: Qf4 — White is better.

35.explanation: Topalov keeps the bishop, but he did not have to do so.

alternatives.label: Strong move
alternatives.note: This was the perfect time to break in the center! Black's king feels drafty, but his central pieces offset this concern.

36.explanation: Kasparov develops the bishop, but it would not be so useful on h3 had Topalov responded accurately.

alternatives.note: again, it was better to recentralize.

37.explanation: Topalov finally strikes out! His king is opened up, but his pieces are activated. Black is even better.

38.explanation: The queen recentralizes with a check.

39.explanation: Topalov's king appears to be fairly safe on a7.

40.explanation: Kasparov finally develops his last piece.

41.explanation: Topalov keeps the e-file from opening and asks the knight to passively retreat.

42.explanation: Kasparov strives to avoid passivity. This is the move which commits Kasparov to a sacrificial path.

43.explanation: This is the correct knight to capture with as the black king has access to b6 now.

46.explanation: A thunderous move! Kasparov sacrifices a rook in the hope of a mating attack.

alternatives.label: Interesting
alternatives.note: This might be the "best" course of action, but only Black can be better in the endgame.

47.explanation: Topalov accepts. Here Topalov gets a lesson as Kasparov's sacrifice proves sound. Declining the sacrifice could have gained a clear advantage.

alternatives.label: Strong move
alternatives.note: This move was much cited after the match. Instead of taking the rook, Black plays a daring king move. An endgame is now unavoidable. Black is better in this endgame.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: Rhe8 Rxe8 Nxe8 Qxf7+ — White is better.

48.explanation: A second rook sacrifice to justify the first! This one cannot be accepted.

alternatives.label: Blunder
alternatives.note: And since White's queen is hanging, the attack falters.

49.explanation: Topalov has no choice but to run the monarch up the board.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: Sideline: Qxe7 Qxd4+ Kb8 Qb6+ Bb7 Nc6+ Ka8 Qa7#.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: Kb8 Qxd4 Nd7 Rxd7 Rxd7 Bxd7 Qxd7 Qxh8+ — White is winning.

50.explanation: The knight on a5 is sacrificed as well to force Topalov's king into a mating net.

alternatives.note: An amazing solution! This stops the queen trades and keeps White's pieces dominating.

alternatives.label: Strong move
alternatives.note: This is even stronger thanks to some great resources.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: What a stunning checkmate!

57.explanation: The only viable way to defend a6 is to give up this bishop.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: Sideline: Rd6 Kb2 Qd4 Qxd4 Rxd4 Rxa6#.

59.explanation: Topalov offers a queen trade and resolves the threat of Qb3#.

60.explanation: Kasparov can win a knight while evading simplification.

61.explanation: Topalov elects to take a pawn.

alternatives.label: Strong move
alternatives.note: This was the last viable defense.

62.explanation: The play is forced from here on.

63.explanation: Topalov has eliminated the dangerous a3-b4 pawns.

64.explanation: Kasparov shows that the attack is not over!

65.explanation: This is the most logical try.

alternatives.label: Mistake
alternatives.note: Sideline: Qxc3 Qxb5+ Ka3 Qa6+ Qa5 Qxa5#.

66.explanation: There is something particularly beautiful about the queen falling back to the corner.

67.explanation: Perhaps the king will be safe on d1?

69.explanation: Topalov's king reaches the opposite rank, and it looks safe on d1!

70.explanation: AMAZING! Kasparov delivers a shot that wins the game. The bishop cannot be taken.

71.explanation: A great try from Topalov! He leaves his queen hanging and counter-attacks.

alternatives.note: Qxf1 Qc2+ Ke1 Re7+ — White is winning.

72.explanation: Kasparov has all the answers. He pins the d2-rook.

73.explanation: Topalov has no choice. He must surrender his queen.

76.explanation: The "sting in the tail" of this long battle.

81.explanation: hoping to attack the kingside pawns.

title: Fischer vs Spassky, Game 6
note: Fischer's celebrated Queen's Gambit win in the 1972 World Championship — Spassky applauded.

title: Capablanca vs Alekhine
note: Two future world champions clash; Capablanca's clarity prevails in a Queen's Gambit.

title: Carlsen vs Gelfand, Tal Memorial
note: At the 2011 Tal Memorial — Carlsen squeezes a Slav structure with modern positional technique.

title: Fischer's Marshall Defense
note: Fischer navigates the sharp Marshall Attack and converts with precise technique.

title: Karpov vs Unzicker, Milan
note: A positional masterpiece — Karpov slowly squeezes a model Ruy Lopez to victory.

title: Carlsen vs Shirov
note: A teenage Carlsen outplays a world-class attacker in a Closed Ruy Lopez.

title: Carlsen vs Anand, Game 11
note: From the 2014 World Championship — Carlsen's Berlin Ruy Lopez win in the final game that retained his title.

title: Tal's Munich Brilliancy
note: Tal vs Tringov — a whirlwind Najdorf attack finished by a stunning queen sacrifice.

title: Tal vs Larsen, Portoroz
note: The Magician from Riga unleashes a typical kingside assault against the Najdorf.

title: Carlsen vs Anand, Game 2
note: A 2014 World Championship Sicilian — Carlsen presses a small edge into a winning attack.

title: Kasparov vs Polugaevsky
note: A young Kasparov demonstrates deep Scheveningen Sicilian preparation and energy.

title: Carlsen vs Caruana, Tiebreak
note: Carlsen squeezes Caruana in a Taimanov Sicilian to sweep the 2018 World Championship rapid tiebreak — annotated by samsh.

0.explanation: White can choose more or less any sensible first move (including 1. e4) in a normal game, or even a must-win game. e4, d4, Nf3, c4- all strike me as having similar chances of fighting for an edge. But if one specifically needs a draw with White, 1.e4 is absolutely the way to do it. Play the most forcing moves!

4.explanation: A bit of a weird move order. c4 only really works against the Kan, but not against the Taimanov

alternatives.note: Now c4 would make a lot more sense. Carlsen played this against Anand in their second match.
This would have avoided the option Black chose in the game.

9.explanation: Definitely the best move under the circumstances

alternatives.note: Has long been known to end in sterile equality very quickly. This is not normally a problem from Black's point of view, but this game is a bit different... The position is very dry and very safe for White. Neither side has any good plans at their disposal to imbalance the game.
Is a better chance to fight for an edge, and White may well be better. But at least Black is keeping some tension in the game.

Black absolutely needed to disrupt White's coordination. after something like Black is not better, and White can complete his development, but at least he doesn't have perfect coordination and a nice space advantage.

14.explanation: Now White develops easily and even maintains a nice edge due to his extra space in the style of the Maroczy bind. My annotations won't be published until the game ends, but at this point, I find it very unlikely we will see a fourth game.

22.explanation: White is incredibly solid. In such structures the best plan for Black is generall to try to force d7-d5 through, but all that does is liquidate the position.

alternatives.note: "Objectively" this has to be the best move, and Black is almost equalizing. But it chops off all the pieces, and this is obviously unacceptable for Caruana. I don't think Magnus will lose a position like this one very often...

34.explanation: White's play is simple and easy. Just keep everything protected and maintain the space advantage. Winning the game will not be easy, but his strong play in the first 2 rapid games earned him the right to sit tight here.

43.explanation: Not much is happening. Black is struggling to find any kind of active plan.

46.explanation: This is undoubtedly the best move, and Magnus must be given credit for being willing to somewhat open his king in a game where a draw clinches the World Championship. White expands his space advantage further.

alternatives.label: Interesting
alternatives.note: White could have gone directly for the king, but one can hardly blame Magnus for looking for simplification and instand gratification. Had this been game 1, I doubt he would hesitate to play g5. And he'd likely have won.
Looks very safe for White.

60.explanation: This looks like the easiest way to force a draw. All the pieces come off.

alternatives.note: Almost keeps some fight, but the bishop on a8 is lost after

65.explanation: Caruana makes the right choice to keep different pieces on the board. White's c-pawn is pretty dangerous and the bishop is better then the knight in it's abolity to send the pawn through, but White's king is open and the knight and queen can be a dangerous attacking pair. White is certainly not worse but Caruana has to take any chance he can get to imbalance the position at all.

78.explanation: The g2 bishop is too strong. Its really hard to imagine how White can get mated.

81.explanation: Black clearly wants f4-f3- it's his only winning attempt. The move is objectively bad but in an absolute must-win scenario, you gotta do what you gotta do.

84.explanation: White's c-pawn will decide the game long before any attack has a dream of coming through.

90.explanation: going for the 3-0 sweep. Magnus showed why he is one of the best players ever... Thanks to all for reading!

alternatives.note: One wonders if Caruana would have repeated moves here. We'll never know because Magnus wanted the whole cake.
